questions.question: What is the time complexity of the CYK algorithm for a string of length n and grammar of size |G|?
questions.explanation: CYK has three nested loops (span, start position, split point) each up to n, giving O(n³), multiplied by grammar size for rule checks.

questions.question: Which property must a grammar satisfy before the CYK algorithm can be applied directly?
options: The grammar must be in Greibach Normal Form
The grammar must be in Chomsky Normal Form
questions.explanation: CYK requires Chomsky Normal Form where every rule is A → BC or A → a.

questions.question: What distinguishes the Earley parser from CYK?
options: Earley can only parse regular languages
Earley handles any CFG without conversion
Earley is always O(n³) regardless of grammar
questions.explanation: Earley's key advantage is handling arbitrary CFGs — including ambiguous and ε-producing grammars — without any conversion.

questions.question: In an Earley item (A → α · β, i), what does the origin index i represent?
options: The current position in the input
The position where the recognition of this item began
questions.explanation: The origin i records where in the input the item's left end was predicted or found, enabling completion to link children back to parents.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the CYK parsing algorithm?
questions.explanation: CYK is a dynamic programming algorithm that fills an n×n table. For each substring (O(n^2) substrings) and each possible split point (O(n) splits), it checks grammar rules. This gives O(n^3 * |G|) where |G| is grammar size — cubic in string length.
